title: Mortality rate attributed to household and ambient air pollution, age-standardized (per 100,000 population)
definition: Mortality rate attributed to household and ambient air pollution is the number of deaths attributable to the joint effects of household and ambient air pollution in a year per 100,000 population. The rates are age-standardized.  Following diseases are taken into account: acute respiratory infections (estimated for all ages); cerebrovascular diseases in adults (estimated above 25 years); ischaemic heart diseases in adults (estimated above 25 years); chronic obstructive pulmonary disease in adults (estimated above 25 years); and lung cancer in adults (estimated above 25 years).

source.license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International unless specifically labeled otherwise by World Bank metadata
source.sourceOrganization: Global Health Observatory Data Repository, World Health Organization (WHO), uri: https://www.who.int/data/gho

editorial.patternNote: The darkest countries include Central African Republic, Lesotho, Solomon Islands, while the lightest include Finland, Norway, Canada; that contrast is the fastest way to read the air pollution mortality pattern.
editorial.whyItMatters: Air pollution mortality combines environment, urbanization, fuel use, and health into a strong expert puzzle.

bestProbeCountries.reason: High anchor: this country sits near the darkest end of the map.
Low anchor: this country clarifies the lightest end of the scale.
Middle check: this country helps separate a true pattern from one-off outliers.

commonConfusions.reason: Particulate emission damage can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
GNI per capita can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.
Health spending per person can tempt players because it shares a strong country ranking with this map, but its unit and definition point to a different story.

editorial.difficultyReason: Expert difficulty: the map can be mistaken for adjacent indicators unless players probe a high, low, and middle country.
regionalSignals: Health maps often separate income, public-health capacity, and conflict exposure.
Check both high-income and low-income anchors.

reviewNotes: Batch 4 promotion: high-interest health/environment crossover with a strong spatial pattern.
Practice-only because mortality-rate maps need careful answer-choice framing.
